anchor.snippet: states that every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers
there will always be exactly four 2s, one 3, two 5s, and no other primes in the product
The requirement that the factors be prime is necessary
every positive integer can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers, up to the order of the factors
if 1 were prime, then factorization into primes would not be unique
The theorem generalizes to other algebraic structures that are called unique factorization domains
if a prime p divides the product ab , then p divides either a or b or both

annotations.label: Every composite has a prime divisor (Elements VII.31)

anchor.snippet: every integer greater than one is divided evenly by some prime number

annotations.label: Every number is prime or has a prime divisor (Elements VII.32)

anchor.snippet: Proposition 32 is derived from proposition 31, and proves that the decomposition is possible
a least common multiple of several prime numbers is not a multiple of any other prime number

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: Every positive integer n > 1 can be represented in exactly one way as a product of prime powers

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: any positive integer can be uniquely represented as an infinite product taken over all the positive prime numbers

anchor.section: Canonical representation of a positive integer
anchor.snippet: Allowing negative exponents provides a canonical form for positive rational numbers

annotations.label: Product, GCD, LCM via canonical representations

anchor.snippet: can be expressed simply in terms of the canonical representations of a and b themselves

annotations.label: Additive and multiplicative functions determined by prime powers

anchor.snippet: the values of additive and multiplicative functions are determined by their values on the powers of prime numbers
If a prime divides the product of two integers, then it must divide at least one of these integers
It must be shown that every integer greater than 1 is either prime or a product of primes
Suppose, to the contrary, there is an integer that has two distinct prime factorizations
The fundamental theorem of arithmetic can also be proved without using Euclid's lemma
the composites have unique factorization as a product of primes
This is the ring of Eisenstein integers , and he proved it has the six units
it was also discovered that unique factorization does not always hold
In algebraic number theory 2 is called irreducible

annotations.label: Prime implies irreducible in integral domains

anchor.snippet: Using these definitions it can be proven that in any integral domain a prime must be irreducible
The rings in which factorization into irreducibles is essentially unique are called unique factorization domains
the rings in which they have unique factorization are called Dedekind domains
There is a version of unique factorization for ordinals

annotations.label: Unique factorization in commutative Möbius monoids

anchor.snippet: Any commutative Möbius monoid satisfies a unique factorization theorem
